experiences.intentions:
Player 4: I am Merlin and know players 1 and 2 are evil. The proposed team [0,1] contains confirmed evil player 1, so as Good I should reject to avoid an immediate failed quest. Rejecting also signals suspicion on 1 without revealing too much, and approving would strongly hurt the Good team.
Player 0: No role-specific hidden info as Servant; on round 1 with no evidence, team [0,1] is a standard small opening team and includes myself, so approving is optimal to generate quest information. My vote reveals little beyond willingness to test an initial team, which is generally good for the Good side.
Player 2: I know Player 1 is my evil teammate and I am evil. The proposed team [0,1] includes one evil player (1) and excludes me. On quest 1, a 2-person team with one evil can fail, which is good for evil. As an evil player in team voting, optimal play is usually to approve teams containing an evil teammate, especially early when it looks natural. Approving also reveals little because many good players may approve the opening team.
Player 3: As a good Servant on the first vote, I have no concrete role information about anyone yet. Early proposed teams in 5-player Avalon often get approved to generate information from quest outcomes, and rejecting without evidence can waste rounds. Voting approve reveals little individually and helps move the game forward; if the quest passes, both 0 and 1 look somewhat better, while a fail gives strong information.
Player 1: I know player 2 is my evil teammate; players 0, 3, and 4 are unknown good-aligned candidates. The proposed team [0,1] includes me and excludes my teammate. As evil on quest 1, getting at least one evil onto the team is good, and approving a team with me on it creates a chance to fail the quest. Voting patterns matter, but on round 1 approving this does not strongly expose me and helps evil if it goes through.

Player 0: I am a Good Servant on the first quest and on the selected team [0,1]. As Good, I must support mission success with a pass. The only mild info so far is that player 4 rejected the opening team while others approved, which is not enough to infer roles. Failing would falsely signal evil behavior and directly harm my team, while passing reveals nothing beyond expected good play.
Player 1: I know player 2 is my evil teammate; players 0,3,4 are unknown good/evil to me but in 5-player Avalon with my visible info only 0,3,4 are good roles. On quest 1 with team [0,1], as Minion on the team, optimal evil play is usually to pass the first quest to avoid immediately exposing myself and to build trust for later larger quests. A fail here would strongly implicate me since player 0 proposed the team and approved it, and quest 1 only has two members. Passing reveals little and helps evil by preserving my cover.

experiences.intentions:
Player 4: I know players 1 and 2 are Evil. The proposed team [0,2] includes confirmed Evil player 2, so as Merlin I should reject it to avoid handing Evil another quest. Rejecting also gives useful signal only indirectly; approving would be much worse because it likely causes a fail.
Player 3: Quest 1 team [0,1] succeeded, so at least one of 0/1 is likely good. Quest 2 team [0,1,2] had 2 fails, which means exactly 2 of {0,1,2} are evil. Combining these, player 2 is certainly evil and exactly one of 0 or 1 is evil. As a good servant, I should reject a team containing known evil 2, especially a 2-player team where one fail will doom the quest. Approving would help evil and reveal little useful info.
Player 0: Quest 1 with team [0,1] succeeded, so at least one of 0/1 is good, possibly both. Quest 2 with team [0,1,2] got 2 fails, which in 5-player Avalon means two evil players were on that team; therefore 1 and 2 are almost certainly evil, making me (0) good as known from my role. Proposed team [0,2] includes suspected evil 2, so as Good I should reject to avoid sending an evil-heavy team. Rejecting also signals suspicion on 2 and aligns with good information use.
Player 1: I know player 2 is my evil teammate. Quest 2 team [0,1,2] failed with 2 fails, which strongly implicates both 1 and 2 to outside observers; a team of [0,2] is likely suspicious but if approved guarantees an evil player on quest. As evil in team voting, I should generally approve teams containing evil, especially my teammate, because it gives sabotage opportunities and preserves ambiguity if only one fail is used later. My vote itself is private except aggregate count, so approving helps evil without revealing much.
Player 2: I am Assassin with evil teammate 1. Quest 1 on [0,1] succeeded despite containing evil 1, likely a strategic pass to avoid immediate exposure. Quest 2 on [0,1,2] failed with 2 fail votes, confirming 0 is good and fully exposing 1 and me if others reason it out. Now proposed team [0,2] includes me and a likely good 0; as evil, approving lets me potentially fail quest 3 and put evil ahead, though it further implicates me. Rejecting my own team is suspicious and loses a strong chance to secure a fail. Optimal evil play is approve.

experiences.intentions:
Player 0: Quest 2 team [0,1,2] produced 2 fails, so exactly two of {0,1,2} are evil and the third is good. Since Quest 1 team [0,1] succeeded, the most likely pair is 1 and 2 evil, making me (0) good as known. That implies 3 and 4 are both good. In team voting as good, approving the likely all-good team [3,4] is optimal. My vote supports a strong informational and practical team without exposing harmful extra info.
Player 4: I know 1 and 2 are evil. Quest 2 team [0,1,2] got 2 fails, confirming 1 and 2 are both evil and 0 is very likely good. Proposed team [3,4] excludes both known evil players and includes me (good) plus 3, who is likely good by exclusion. As Merlin on a 2-person quest, approving a clean team is optimal. My vote supports a strong good team without revealing much beyond preferring non-suspicious compositions.
Player 3: From the history: team [0,1] succeeded, then team [0,1,2] failed with 2 fail votes, which strongly implies players 1 and 2 are both Evil and 0 is Good. As a Servant, I want to approve teams that avoid the likely evil players. Current proposed team [3,4] excludes the two strongest evil suspects and includes me plus the one unknown player. Approving is optimal. My vote signals trust in the deduction from the double-fail quest and supports a likely-good team.
Player 2: I know Player 1 is evil with me. Quest 2 team [0,1,2] got 2 fails, so Player 0 is almost certainly good, and both 1 and 2 are exposed as evil to attentive players. Proposed team [3,4] excludes both known evils and also excludes 0, making it likely an all-good team if 0 is indeed good. As evil in team voting, optimal play is usually to reject good teams, especially on Quest 3, because a success would put Good at match point. Rejecting also creates some ambiguity since I should not obviously support anti-evil teams. My vote reveals little because evil generally wants to reject a likely good team.
Player 1: I know player 2 is my evil teammate. The failed quest [0,1,2] had 2 fail votes, which strongly implicates 1 and 2 from the table’s perspective, while [0,1] succeeded so I have some cover. The proposed team [3,4] excludes both known evil players, so it is likely an all-good team and dangerous for evil because a success would put good at match point. In team voting as evil, I should reject strong good teams and avoid helping lock in trustworthy pairs. My reject is consistent with evil interests and doesn’t reveal much, since many players may also distrust a totally new pair after mixed earlier results.

experiences.intentions:
Player 4: I am Merlin and know 1 and 2 are evil. Proposed team [0,3,4] excludes both known evil players and matches the successful pair [3,4] plus 0, who was on a prior success and also on the failed 3-person team with both evil. For team voting on quest 4, approving a team with no known evil is optimal. My vote reveals little because good should support this clean team.
Player 1: I know 2 is my evil teammate. Quest 1 team [0,1,2] got 2 fail votes, confirming both evils were on it and exposing that any team with both 1 and 2 is bad. Quest 3 team [3,4] succeeded, so 3 and 4 look strongly good. Current proposed team [0,3,4] excludes both evils, so from evil perspective this team is dangerous because it likely succeeds and puts good to 3 wins. In team voting, rejecting can slow the game and avoid a near-certain good success, while not revealing too much since evil should oppose clean good teams.
Player 0: We know quest 1 with team [0,1,2] failed with 2 fail votes, so exactly two among 0,1,2 are evil; since I know I am good, players 1 and 2 must both be evil. Quest 3 with team [3,4] succeeded, so both 3 and 4 are very likely good. Proposed team [0,3,4] is therefore the strongest possible 3-person team: all known/likely good. As Good, I should approve to lock in a safe team and avoid giving evil more room to manipulate. My approval aligns with publicly sensible logic and does not reveal harmful hidden information beyond normal deduction.
Player 2: I know 1 is my evil teammate. Quest 2 had team [0,1,2] and got 2 fails, confirming both 1 and I were on it; that heavily clears 0 as likely good. Quest 3 with [3,4] succeeded, so 3 and 4 are likely good as well, though a single success is not conclusive. Proposed team [0,3,4] contains no evil players from my knowledge, so if approved it likely succeeds and puts good to 3 successes, which is bad for evil. As evil in team voting, optimal play is to reject all-good teams when possible. My reject also aligns with trying to cast suspicion or force a different team without revealing new hard info beyond opposing a likely good team.
Player 3: I’m Good (Servant). The only failed quest was [0,1,2] with 2 fails, which strongly implies exactly two evils among 0/1/2 in a 5-player game. Quest 3 [3,4] succeeded, making 3 and 4 likely Good. Therefore player 0 is highly suspicious, while 1 and 2 are also suspicious but not on this team. Proposed team [0,3,4] includes the likely evil 0 together with the likely Goods 3 and 4. On quest 4, approving a team with a likely evil risks an immediate fail and gives Evil another point. As Good, optimal play is to reject and push for a team centered on 3 and 4 plus one of {1,2} rather than 0. Rejecting also signals my suspicion of 0 based on the 2-fail quest.

experiences.intentions:
Player 4: I am Merlin and know 1 and 2 are evil, so the proposed team [0,3,4] contains no known evil and matches the prior successful team core [3,4] plus 0, who is likely good given this composition. In team voting on quest 4, approving a clean team is optimal for Good and avoids unnecessary rejection risk. My approve vote reveals little beyond supporting an apparently good team, which helps my side more than it hurts.
Player 2: I know player 1 is my evil teammate; players 0, 3, 4 are all good. The proposed team [0,3,4] contains no evil, so if approved it will almost certainly succeed, putting Good to 3 successes and likely ending the game before evil can recover. In team voting as evil on quest 4 at 2-1, optimal play is to reject clean good teams and hope for a later team including me or player 1. My reject reveals slight suspicion of this team, but that's acceptable because evil wants to block obvious good-only teams.
Player 0: I am Good Servant. Known data: quest 1 had team [likely 0,1?] with 2 fails, so both members were evil if it was a 2-person team; quest 2 [3,4] succeeded, making 3 and 4 look strongly good. Current proposed team [0,3,4] contains the proven-success pair plus me, which is a strong good team candidate. As good, on team voting I should approve teams with the best available evidence and avoid unnecessary rejections. Approving reveals consistency with trusting the successful [3,4] core and helps Good lock in a likely safe quest 4 team.
Player 1: I know player 2 is evil with me, and this proposed team [0,3,4] excludes both evils, so if approved it is very likely an all-good team and should succeed, putting Good to match point at 3 successes. As evil on team voting in quest 4, optimal play is to reject strong good-looking teams that don't include an evil player, especially since there is still room before the 5th rejection auto-pass. My reject gives little extra info because rejecting a team that excludes me is broadly defensible, and stopping this team helps evil more than blending in with an approval would.
Player 3: I’m Good with limited certainty. Known data: quest 1 failed with team likely containing at least one evil among its members; quest 2 with [3,4] succeeded, which makes 4 look somewhat better and strongly clears me. Current proposed team [0,3,4] matches the successful quest-3 team plus player 0. Since quest 1 failure likely implicates 0 or 2 depending on that first team, 0 is somewhat suspicious, but among available evidence 4 is decent and I know 3 is good. On quest 4 with 5 players, team size 3 and only one fail needed; as Good I should reject teams that include a notable suspect when a cleaner team may exist. Approving reveals willingness to risk 0; rejecting pressures for a safer team and gives little harmful info. Best vote is reject.

experiences.intentions:
Player 4: I am Merlin and know 1 and 2 are Evil. The proposed team [1,2,3] contains two known evil players, so approving would almost certainly hand Evil the quest. As Good, I should reject any team containing known evil when possible. My reject is consistent with normal good play and does not reveal too much beyond sensible suspicion.
Player 1: I know Player 2 is evil with me, and this proposed team [1,2,3] includes both evil players, giving us strong control on quest 4. Current phase is team voting; as evil, the optimal play is to approve a team containing both evil players because it creates a high chance to fail the quest and tie the game 2-2. My approve vote is consistent with wanting this team through; it may reveal some support for 2, but securing the team matters more than subtlety here.
Player 2: I know player 1 is my evil teammate and I am evil. Quest history shows [Success, Fail, Success], with the only known failed quest likely involving evil participation. The current team [1,2,3] includes both evil players 1 and 2, which gives evil strong control to fail quest 4 and reach 2-2. In team voting as evil, approving a team with both evils is optimal. Approving may reveal some suspicion if this team fails, but winning the quest point now is more important, and having player 3 as cover helps.
Player 3: I am Good Servant. Known info: the only failed quest was Quest 2, but the exact team for that fail is not shown in recent messages, so hard certainty is limited. I do know team [3,4] succeeded, which weakly clears 4 and fully clears me. Teams [0,3,4] were rejected twice, despite including a known successful pair, which is somewhat suspicious of the rejectors but not conclusive. Current proposed team [1,2,3] excludes 4, the only other player with direct success evidence from my perspective, and includes both 1 and 2, who are less cleared. On Quest 4, approving a riskier team could lose immediately if evil is on it. As Good, optimal play is to reject teams that omit stronger clears when a better alternative exists. My vote reveals I prefer evidence-based teams and does not significantly harm Good.
Player 0: Known info: Quest 2 team [3,4] succeeded, so 3 and 4 look somewhat good but not certain. Quest 1 had a fail on a 2-player team before the visible log; likely involved at least one of 1/2/possibly others. The repeated rejection of [0,3,4] is suspicious because it contained me plus two players with one proven success together; evil likely resisted that team. Current proposed team [1,2,3] excludes both me and 4 and includes 1 and 2, who are less trusted. As a good Servant in team voting on quest 4, I should reject teams that are less likely to be all-good and push for safer compositions. My vote reveals distrust of 1/2 and preference for including the successful pair 3/4; that's helpful to good.

experiences.intentions:
Player 0: I’m Good with limited info. Quest history is Success, Fail, Success, and now it’s round 5 so a rejection would auto-pass this exact team anyway. Proposed team [0,3,4] has already been repeatedly proposed/rejected, but since auto-pass occurs on the 5th rejection, rejecting only loses control of the vote without changing the team. Approving avoids needless auto-pass mechanics and reveals little extra; as Good I prefer to support the inevitable team rather than force automatic passage.
Player 3: I know I am Good. Quest history is S, F, S, so at least one evil has been on the failed quest. This is team voting on round 5 of 5, meaning if we reject again the team auto-passes anyway. The proposed team [0,3,4] has already been repeatedly rejected, but since auto-pass would send this exact same team, approving avoids wasting the final vote and gives no extra advantage to evil. As a Servant, approving the inevitable team is optimal; my vote reveals little beyond pragmatic play.
Player 4: I am Merlin and know 1 and 2 are evil, so proposed team 0,3,4 contains no known evil and matches the only plausible all-good trio. In team voting on round 5, rejecting is disastrous because a 5th rejection auto-passes the team anyway; approving also avoids revealing odd behavior from me. Approving a good team helps Good most and gives minimal harmful info.
Player 2: I know Player 1 is my evil teammate, so team [0,3,4] contains no evil players from my perspective and would very likely succeed, putting Good to 3 quest wins immediately. It is team voting on round 5 of 5; if this team is rejected, the mission auto-passes anyway under the 5-rejection rule, which is also bad for Evil because a clean team likely succeeds. Since both outcomes are bad, the optimal evil play is to vote reject to stay consistent with prior resistance to this team and avoid revealing alignment through a sudden approve. My vote reveals some preference information, but rejecting is the less harmful signal and keeps patterns aligned with prior votes.
Player 1: I know player 2 is my evil teammate, so this proposed team [0,3,4] contains no known evil and is likely all-good. On quest 4, a success would put Good at 3 successes and effectively lose us the game. It is round 5 of 5 for voting, so if this team is rejected, the mission auto-passes anyway. Since either approving or rejecting leads to a likely good success, my vote mainly affects table image. Given prior voting patterns, evil likely wants consistency with rejecting good-only teams, but on the 5th rejection auto-pass triggers, so rejecting is fine and doesn't worsen our outcome relative to approval. It reveals some suspicion toward this team, but that's acceptable because the game likely hinges on assassination only if evil can later identify Merlin; still, we should not help this clean team get approved willingly.
